stops.name: Cathédrale Notre-Dame de Paris
stops.shortDescription: A masterpiece of French Gothic architecture consecrated in 1189. Famous for its gargoyles and flying buttresses, it is currently undergoing a massive restoration following the 2019 fire.

stops.name: Sainte-Chapelle
stops.shortDescription: Commissioned by King Louis IX to house his collection of Passion relics, including Christ's Crown of Thorns. It boasts one of the most extensive and breathtaking 13th-century stained glass collections in the world.

stops.name: Pont Neuf
stops.shortDescription: Ironically translating to 'New Bridge', this is actually the oldest standing bridge across the river Seine in Paris, completed in 1607. It was the first stone bridge in Paris not to have houses built upon it.

stops.name: Louvre Museum
stops.shortDescription: The world's largest art museum, originally built as a fortress in the late 12th century. It is famously home to the Mona Lisa and the striking glass pyramid designed by I. M. Pei.

stops.name: Palais Royal
stops.shortDescription: Originally the personal residence of Cardinal Richelieu, this palace features beautiful public gardens. Its inner courtyard is famous for 'Les Deux Plateaux', a controversial art installation of black-and-white striped columns.

stops.name: Jardin des Tuileries
stops.shortDescription: Created by Catherine de' Medici as the garden of the Tuileries Palace in 1564, it became a public park after the French Revolution. It separates the Louvre from the Place de la Concorde.

stops.name: Place de la Concorde
stops.shortDescription: The largest public square in Paris, notorious as the execution site of Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette during the French Revolution. At its center stands a 3,300-year-old Egyptian obelisk from the Luxor Temple.

stops.name: Pont Alexandre III
stops.shortDescription: Widely regarded as the most ornate and extravagant bridge in Paris. It features exuberant Art Nouveau lamps, cherubs, and golden equestrian statues.

stops.name: Grand Palais
stops.shortDescription: A large historic site, exhibition hall and museum complex recognizable by its massive glass barrel-vaulted roof. It was constructed for the Universal Exposition of 1900.

stops.name: Petit Palais
stops.shortDescription: Built across from the Grand Palais, this elegant building now houses the City of Paris Museum of Fine Arts, offering a beautiful semi-circular courtyard and garden.

stops.name: Avenue des Champs-Élysées
stops.shortDescription: One of the world's most famous commercial streets, stretching nearly two kilometers. It's lined with luxury shops, cafes, and theaters, leading up to the Arc de Triomphe.

stops.name: Arc de Triomphe
stops.shortDescription: A monumental arch honoring those who fought and died for France in the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars. The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier lies beneath its vault.

stops.name: Place du Trocadéro
stops.shortDescription: An expansive esplanade offering the most iconic, unobstructed views of the Eiffel Tower across the Seine. It is a favorite spot for photographers and visitors alike.

stops.name: Eiffel Tower
stops.shortDescription: The global cultural icon of France, constructed from wrought iron by Gustave Eiffel for the 1889 World's Fair. It remains one of the most recognizable structures in the world.
